steps.title: Identify the appositive structure
steps.content: The blank contains an appositive—a noun phrase where “Stephen Graham Jones” renames “novelist.” The sentence structure is: “turned to the novelist [appositive] to craft...” The key is determining whether this appositive should be set off by commas.

steps.title: Apply the appositive rule
steps.content:
A restrictive appositive (specifying which person) takes no commas; a non-restrictive appositive (providing extra information) takes commas on both sides.

Here, “Stephen Graham Jones” identifies which novelist the editors consulted, making it restrictive—no commas needed.

Additionally, the infinitive phrase “to craft” must follow without a comma, which is standard for expressions of purpose.

Choice B correctly applies both rules. A adds an incorrect comma before “to craft,” C adds unnecessary commas treating the appositive as non-restrictive, and D creates an asymmetrical comma pattern.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
